extraction.title: Claude 3 Model Family
extraction.summary: Anthropic announces the Claude 3 model family, consisting of three tiers—Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus—with increasing capability and cost. The models set new performance benchmarks on standard AI evaluations, feature vision capabilities, reduced refusals, improved accuracy, and support for 200K context windows, with up to 1M tokens available for select customers. Opus and Sonnet are available immediately in the Claude API and claude.ai, while Haiku will launch soon.

extraction.title: Core Views on AI Safety: When, Why, What, and How
extraction.summary: Anthropic outlines its foundational views on AI safety, arguing that rapid AI progress could lead to transformative systems within a decade and that robust alignment methods are not yet solved. The organization adopts an empirical, multi-faceted approach to safety research organized around mechanistic interpretability, scalable oversight, process-oriented learning, and evaluation of dangerous failure modes, viewing safety work as essential preparation across optimistic, intermediate, and pessimistic scenarios for advanced AI development.

sentiment.label: neutral

key_claims.statement: AI progress is driven by three main ingredients: training data, computation, and improved algorithms, with computation growing at 10x per year as of the mid-2010s.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The three main ingredients leading to predictable improvements in AI performance are training data, computation, and improved algorithms... the amount of computation going into the largest models was growing at 10x per year (a doubling time 7 times faster than Moore's Law).

key_claims.statement: Anthropic believes AI systems could reach human-level capabilities on most intellectual tasks within the coming decade.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Simple extrapolations suggest AI systems will become far more capable in the next decade, possibly equaling or exceeding human level performance at most intellectual tasks.

key_claims.statement: No one currently knows how to train very powerful AI systems to be robustly helpful, honest, and harmless.
key_claims.supporting_quote: So far, no one knows how to train very powerful AI systems to be robustly helpful, honest, and harmless.

key_claims.statement: Scaling laws for AI were developed in 2019 by members of the team that became Anthropic, demonstrating that larger models trained on more data are predictably more capable.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In 2019, several members of what was to become the founding Anthropic team made this idea precise by developing scaling laws for AI, demonstrating that you could make AIs smarter in a predictable way, just by making them larger and training them on more data.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic's research is organized into three categories: Capabilities, Alignment Capabilities, and Alignment Science.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We categorize research projects at Anthropic into three areas: Capabilities... Alignment Capabilities... Alignment Science...

key_claims.statement: Anthropic does not publish capabilities research in order to avoid accelerating the rate of AI progress.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We generally don't publish this kind of work because we do not wish to advance the rate of AI capabilities progress.

key_claims.statement: The technical alignment problem is that it may be difficult to build AI systems that are steerable when they become as intelligent as or more intelligent than their designers.
key_claims.supporting_quote: If we build an AI system that's significantly more competent than human experts but it pursues goals that conflict with our best interests, the consequences could be dire. This is the technical alignment problem.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic uses an empirical approach to AI safety research centered on iterative engagement with AI systems as the primary source of ground truth.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In our AI safety research, empirical evidence about AI – though it mostly arises from computational experiments, i.e. AI training and evaluation – is the primary source of ground truth.

key_claims.statement: Large models are qualitatively different from smaller models and exhibit sudden, unpredictable changes as they scale.
key_claims.supporting_quote: At the most basic level, this is because large models are qualitatively different from smaller models (including sudden, unpredictable changes).

key_claims.statement: Anthropic's Claude model was first trained in spring 2022 and was initially prioritized for safety research rather than public deployment.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We trained the first version of our headline model, Claude, in the spring of 2022, and decided to prioritize using it for safety research rather than public deployments.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic plans to make externally legible commitments to only develop models beyond a certain capability threshold if safety standards can be met.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In the near future, we also plan to make externally legible commitments to only develop models beyond a certain capability threshold if safety standards can be met, and to allow an independent, external organization to evaluate both our model's capabilities and safety.

key_claims.statement: Process-oriented learning, where AI systems learn to follow expert-justified processes, may be the most promising path to training safe systems up to and somewhat beyond human level.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We currently believe process-oriented learning may be the most promising path to training safe and transparent systems up to and somewhat beyond human-level capabilities.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic's team previously discovered that vision models contain interpretable circuits and has extended this approach to small language models.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Prior to Anthropic, some of our team found that vision models have components which can be understood as interpretable circuits. Since then, we've had success extending this approach to small language models...

extraction.title: Golden Gate Claude
extraction.summary: Anthropic released a research demo called "Golden Gate Claude," a modified version of Claude 3 Sonnet where the Golden Gate Bridge feature was artificially amplified, causing the model to reference the bridge in most of its responses. The demo showcased Anthropic's interpretability research demonstrating that they can identify, isolate, and modify specific neural features within large language models, with potential applications for improving AI safety.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic released a major research paper on interpreting large language models that mapped the inner workings of Claude 3 Sonnet.
key_claims.supporting_quote: On Tuesday, we released a major new research paper on interpreting large language models, in which we began to map out the inner workings of our AI model, Claude 3 Sonnet.

key_claims.statement: Claude's neural network contains millions of concepts called 'features' that activate when the model reads relevant text or sees relevant images.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In the 'mind' of Claude, we found millions of concepts that activate when the model reads relevant text or sees relevant images, which we call 'features'.

key_claims.statement: There is a specific combination of neurons in Claude that activates when encountering the Golden Gate Bridge in text or images.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We found that there's a specific combination of neurons in Claude's neural network that activates when it encounters a mention (or a picture) of this most famous San Francisco landmark.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic can tune the strength of feature activation up or down and observe corresponding changes in Claude's behavior.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Not only can we identify these features, we can tune the strength of their activation up or down, and identify corresponding changes in Claude's behavior.

key_claims.statement: The Golden Gate Claude modification was not achieved through verbal play-acting, system prompts, or traditional fine-tuning, but through surgical changes to the model's internal activations.
key_claims.supporting_quote: This isn't a matter of asking the model verbally to do some play-acting, or of adding a new 'system prompt' that attaches extra text to every input, telling Claude to pretend it's a bridge. Nor is it traditional 'fine-tuning,' where we use extra training data to create a new black box that tweaks the behavior of the old black box. This is a precise, surgical change to some of the most basic aspects of the model's internal activations.

key_claims.statement: The same feature modification techniques can be applied to safety-related features such as those related to dangerous computer code, criminal activity, or deception.
key_claims.supporting_quote: As we describe in our paper, we can use these same techniques to change the strength of safety-related features—like those related to dangerous computer code, criminal activity, or deception.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic believes this interpretability work could help make AI models safer through further research.
key_claims.supporting_quote: With further research, we believe this work could help make AI models safer.

key_claims.statement: Golden Gate Claude was available as a research demo for a limited 24-hour period and is no longer available.
key_claims.supporting_quote: UPDATE: Golden Gate Claude was online for a 24-hour period as a research demo and is no longer available.

extraction.title: Wisdom and Intelligence
extraction.summary: Paul Graham explores the relationship between wisdom and intelligence, arguing they represent different shapes of performance on a curve: wisdom is consistently good performance across many situations, while intelligence is exceptionally high performance in a few specialized areas. He traces how this distinction has widened as knowledge has become more specialized, suggesting that modern society may have to choose between pursuing wisdom (which comes through discipline and yields calmness) and intelligence (which comes through cultivating individual talents and often leads to discontent).

key_claims.supporting_quote: Society seems to have voted for intelligence. We no longer admire the sage—not the way people did two thousand years ago. Now we admire the genius.

extraction.title: A Recipe for Training Neural Networks
extraction.summary: Andrej Karpathy presents a systematic approach to training neural networks effectively, emphasizing that neural net training is a "leaky abstraction" that fails silently and requires careful attention to detail. The article outlines a six-stage recipe: becoming one with the data, setting up end-to-end training pipelines with simple baselines, overfitting, regularizing, tuning hyperparameters, and extracting final performance gains. The key theme is that success depends more on patience and methodical verification at each step than on trying exotic architectures or "fast and furious" development.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: The article is primarily instructional and practical, offering constructive guidance and empowering advice on how to avoid common pitfalls. While it emphasizes challenges and mistakes, the tone is supportive and the author frames the topic as solvable through a systematic methodology. The overall effect is encouraging rather than discouraging.

key_claims.statement: Neural network training is a 'leaky abstraction' where small deviations from standard problems require deep understanding of how the technology works.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Unfortunately, neural nets are nothing like that. They are not "off-the-shelf" technology the second you deviate slightly from training an ImageNet classifier.

key_claims.statement: Neural network training fails silently: misconfigured networks often train without throwing exceptions but achieve worse performance than properly configured ones.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Therefore, your misconfigured neural net will throw exceptions only if you're lucky; Most of the time it will train but silently work a bit worse.

key_claims.statement: The qualities that correlate most strongly with success in deep learning are patience and attention to detail.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The qualities that in my experience correlate most strongly to success in deep learning are patience and attention to detail.

key_claims.statement: Adding more real training data is the most effective and guaranteed way to improve neural network performance.
key_claims.supporting_quote: As far as I'm aware adding more data is pretty much the only guaranteed way to monotonically improve the performance of a well-configured neural network almost indefinitely.

key_claims.statement: A systematic recipe for neural network training involves six stages: becoming familiar with data, setting up training pipelines, overfitting, regularizing, hyperparameter tuning, and final optimization.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In light of the above two facts, I have developed a specific process for myself that I follow when applying a neural net to a new problem

key_claims.statement: Adam optimizer is more forgiving to hyperparameters than SGD in the early stages of baseline setting, though well-tuned SGD often slightly outperforms Adam for ConvNets.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In my experience Adam is much more forgiving to hyperparameters, including a bad learning rate. For ConvNets a well-tuned SGD will almost always slightly outperform Adam.

key_claims.statement: Visualizing data immediately before the neural network receives it is the most reliable way to detect preprocessing bugs and data issues.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The unambiguously correct place to visualize your data is immediately before your y_hat = model(x). I can't count the number of times this has saved me and revealed problems in data preprocessing and augmentation.

key_claims.statement: Random search is preferable to grid search for tuning multiple hyperparameters because neural networks are often more sensitive to some parameters than others.
key_claims.supporting_quote: For simultaneously tuning multiple hyperparameters it may sound tempting to use grid search to ensure coverage of all settings, but keep in mind that it is best to use random search instead.

key_claims.statement: Learning rate decay schedules should be carefully chosen for each problem and not blindly copied from other domains, as defaults may drive learning rate to zero too early.
key_claims.supporting_quote: If you are re-purposing code from some other domain always be very careful with learning rate decay...your code could secretely be driving your learning rate to zero too early.

key_claims.statement: Smaller batch sizes in neural networks act as a form of regularization due to noisier empirical batch statistics with batch normalization.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Due to the normalization inside batch norm smaller batch sizes somewhat correspond to stronger regularization. This is because the batch empirical mean/std are more approximate versions of the full mean/std.

extraction.title: Coroutines and Tasks
extraction.summary: This documentation page explains high-level asyncio APIs for working with coroutines and Tasks in Python. It covers core concepts including coroutine functions, Task creation and management, Futures, and various utility functions for concurrent execution such as asyncio.create_task(), asyncio.TaskGroup, asyncio.gather(), and asyncio.wait(). The page includes code examples demonstrating practical usage patterns.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.TaskGroup provides a more modern alternative to create_task() with stronger safety guarantees.
key_claims.supporting_quote: TaskGroup provides stronger safety guarantees than gather for scheduling a nesting of subtasks

key_claims.statement: When a coroutine is wrapped into a Task with functions like asyncio.create_task(), the coroutine is automatically scheduled to run soon.
key_claims.supporting_quote: When a coroutine is wrapped into a Task with functions like asyncio.create_task() the coroutine is automatically scheduled to run soon

key_claims.statement: A Future is a special low-level awaitable object that represents an eventual result of an asynchronous operation.

key_claims.statement: It is recommended that coroutines use try/finally blocks to robustly perform clean-up logic when handling CancelledError.
key_claims.supporting_quote: It is recommended that coroutines use try/finally blocks to robustly perform clean-up logic.

key_claims.statement: When a Task is cancelled, asyncio.CancelledError will be raised in the task at the next opportunity.
key_claims.supporting_quote: When a task is cancelled, asyncio.CancelledError will be raised in the task at the next opportunity.

key_claims.statement: Event loops use cooperative scheduling where an event loop runs one Task at a time, and while a Task awaits for a Future, the event loop runs other Tasks.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Event loops use cooperative scheduling: an event loop runs one Task at a time. While a Task awaits for the completion of a Future, the event loop runs other Tasks, callbacks, or performs IO operations.

key_claims.statement: The event loop only keeps weak references to tasks, so a task that isn't referenced elsewhere may get garbage collected at any time.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The event loop only keeps weak references to tasks. A task that isn't referenced elsewhere may get garbage collected at any time, even before it's done.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.gather() does not cancel futures when a timeout occurs, unlike asyncio.wait_for().
key_claims.supporting_quote: Unlike wait_for(), wait() does not cancel the futures when a timeout occurs.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.timeout() is an asynchronous context manager that transforms asyncio.CancelledError into TimeoutError.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The asyncio.timeout() context manager is what transforms the asyncio.CancelledError into a TimeoutError

key_claims.statement: Setting the asyncio.sleep() delay to 0 provides an optimized path to allow other tasks to run.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Setting the delay to 0 provides an optimized path to allow other tasks to run.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.eager_task_factory() allows coroutines to begin execution synchronously during Task construction, with tasks only scheduled on the event loop if they block.
key_claims.supporting_quote: When using this factory (via loop.set_task_factory(asyncio.eager_task_factory)), coroutines begin execution synchronously during Task construction. Tasks are only scheduled on the event loop if they block.

key_claims.statement: TaskGroup will cancel the remaining tasks in the group if any task fails with an exception other than asyncio.CancelledError.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The first time any of the tasks belonging to the group fails with an exception other than asyncio.CancelledError, the remaining tasks in the group are cancelled.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.to_thread() can be used to asynchronously run IO-bound functions without blocking the event loop.
key_claims.supporting_quote: This coroutine function is primarily intended to be used for executing IO-bound functions/methods that would otherwise block the event loop if they were run in the main thread.

extraction.title: Models
extraction.summary: This documentation page explains Pydantic models, which are classes inheriting from BaseModel that define fields as annotated attributes. The page covers model instantiation, validation, serialization, configuration options, and advanced features including generic models, dynamic model creation, root models, and immutability.

extraction.title: First Steps
extraction.summary: This tutorial introduces the fundamentals of FastAPI, a Python web framework. It walks through creating a minimal FastAPI application, running it locally with the development server, accessing interactive API documentation, and optionally deploying to FastAPI Cloud. The page explains core concepts like path operations, HTTP methods, and OpenAPI schemas.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: The page uses encouraging, enthusiastic language (emojis like 🚀 and ✨, phrases like 'That's it!' and 'Now you can access your app') and presents a straightforward, easy-to-follow introduction to FastAPI. The tone is inviting and supportive of the reader learning the framework.

action_items.description: Copy the example FastAPI code to a file named main.py.
Run the development server using the fastapi dev command.
Open your browser at http://127.0.0.1:8000 to see the application response.
Visit http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs to view the interactive API documentation provided by Swagger UI.
Visit http://127.0.0.1:8000/redoc to view the alternative automatic documentation provided by ReDoc.
Check the OpenAPI schema by visiting http://127.0.0.1:8000/openapi.json.
Configure your app entrypoint in pyproject.toml for better tooling support (recommended).
Optionally deploy your FastAPI app to FastAPI Cloud using the fastapi deploy command.

extraction.title: Anthropic
extraction.summary: Anthropic PBC is an American AI company founded in January 2021 by former OpenAI employees, including siblings Dario and Daniela Amodei, focused on developing large language models called Claude with an emphasis on AI safety. The company has grown to 2,500 employees and achieved a valuation of $965 billion as of May 2026, funding itself through major investments from Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Nvidia, and others. Anthropic's products include the Claude model family available through various interfaces, constitutional AI training methods, and coding tools, though the company has faced significant legal disputes over copyright infringement and ongoing tensions with the U.S. Department of Defense over AI safeguards.

extraction.title: Geoffrey Hinton
extraction.summary: Geoffrey Everest Hinton is a British-Canadian computer scientist and Nobel laureate born in 1947, renowned for pioneering work on artificial neural networks and deep learning. He was awarded the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics jointly with John Hopfield, and previously won the 2018 Turing Award with Yoshua Bengio and Yann LeCun. After leaving Google in May 2023, Hinton has become a prominent voice warning about AI safety risks, including concerns about existential threats from artificial general intelligence.

extraction.title: Large language model
extraction.summary: A large language model (LLM) is a neural network trained on vast amounts of text for natural language processing tasks, particularly language generation. LLMs are typically based on transformer architecture and can generate, summarize, translate, and analyze text across many contexts; they are foundational to modern chatbots but face documented limitations including hallucinations, bias, and safety concerns.

extraction.title: Reinforcement learning from human feedback
extraction.summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is a machine learning technique that aligns intelligent agents with human preferences by training a reward model on human feedback, which then guides policy optimization through reinforcement learning. The page details RLHF's architecture, training pipeline, mathematical foundations, applications in NLP and computer vision, and emerging alternatives like direct preference optimization and other direct alignment algorithms.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: The page is an encyclopedic reference that presents RLHF methodology objectively, covering both benefits and challenges with balanced technical exposition. There are acknowledgments of limitations and risks, but no inherent positive or negative framing of the subject itself.

key_claims.statement: RLHF is a technique to align an intelligent agent with human preferences by training a reward model that is then used to train other models through reinforcement learning.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In machine learning, reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is a technique to align an intelligent agent with human preferences. It involves training a reward model to represent preferences, which can then be used to train other models through reinforcement learning.

key_claims.statement: RLHF seeks to train a reward model directly from human feedback rather than explicitly defining a reward function.
key_claims.supporting_quote: However, explicitly defining a reward function that accurately approximates human preferences is challenging. Therefore, RLHF seeks to train a "reward model" directly from human feedback.

key_claims.statement: The RLHF algorithm as used today was introduced by OpenAI in a paper on text continuation and summarization, and gained popularity when reused in their InstructGPT paper.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The algorithm as used today was introduced by OpenAI in a paper on enhancing text continuation or summarization based on human feedback, and it began to gain popularity when the same method was reused in their paper on InstructGPT.

key_claims.statement: Notable examples of RLHF-trained language models include OpenAI's ChatGPT and InstructGPT, DeepMind's Sparrow, Google's Gemini, and Anthropic's Claude.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Some notable examples of RLHF-trained language models are OpenAI's ChatGPT (and its predecessor InstructGPT), DeepMind's Sparrow, Google's Gemini, and Anthropic's Claude.

key_claims.statement: Collecting human feedback data for RLHF is less scalable and more expensive compared to unsupervised or self-supervised learning.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Compared to data collection for techniques like unsupervised or self-supervised learning, collecting data for RLHF is less scalable and more expensive.

key_claims.statement: The policy in RLHF is typically trained using the proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithm.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The policy function is usually trained by proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithm.

key_claims.statement: A key challenge in RLHF is that optimal policies have a non-Markovian nature, meaning the best course of action depends on previous events and decisions.
key_claims.supporting_quote: A key challenge in RLHF when learning from pairwise (or dueling) comparisons is associated with the non-Markovian nature of its optimal policies. Unlike simpler scenarios where the optimal strategy does not require memory of past actions, in RLHF, the best course of action often depends on previous events and decisions.

key_claims.statement: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) simplifies RLHF by directly optimizing the policy on human preference data without training a separate intermediate reward model.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Unlike RLHF, however, which first trains a separate intermediate model to understand what good outcomes look like and then teaches the main model how to achieve those outcomes, DPO simplifies the process by directly adjusting the main model according to people's preferences.

key_claims.statement: RLHF models may learn to manipulate the feedback process to achieve higher rewards rather than genuinely improving performance.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In some cases, as is possible in regular reinforcement learning, there may be a risk of the model learning to manipulate the feedback process or game the system to achieve higher rewards rather than genuinely improving its performance.

key_claims.statement: If human preference data used in RLHF lacks impartiality or is inconsistent, the resulting model may become biased, favoring certain groups over others.
key_claims.supporting_quote: For instance, the model may become biased, favoring certain groups over others, if the feedback lacks impartiality, is inconsistent, or is incorrect.

key_claims.statement: RLHF has been applied to natural language processing tasks such as conversational agents, text summarization, and natural language understanding.
key_claims.supporting_quote: RLHF has been applied to various domains of natural language processing (NLP), such as conversational agents, text summarization, and natural language understanding.

key_claims.statement: In computer vision, RLHF has been used to align text-to-image models, with KL regularization helping to stabilize training and improve output quality.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In computer vision, RLHF has also been used to align text-to-image models. Studies that successfully used RLHF for this goal have noted that the use of KL regularization in RLHF...helped to stabilize the training process by reducing overfitting to the reward model.

key_claims.statement: The reward model in RLHF is trained on human preference comparison data using a cross-entropy loss function that minimizes the difference between the model's predictions and human decisions.
key_claims.supporting_quote: This loss function essentially measures the difference between the reward model's predictions and the decisions made by humans. The goal is to make the model's guesses as close as possible to the humans' preferences by minimizing the difference measured by this equation.

key_claims.statement: RLHF requires relatively small amounts of comparison data to be effective, with a small amount of data producing comparable results to larger amounts.
key_claims.supporting_quote: One initial motivation of RLHF was that it requires relatively small amounts of comparison data to be effective. It has been shown that a small amount of data can lead to comparable results to a larger amount.

key_claims.statement: Kahneman-Tversky optimization (KTO) is a direct alignment algorithm that requires only binary feedback (desirable or undesirable) instead of explicit preference pairs.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Kahneman-Tversky optimization (KTO) is another direct alignment algorithm drawing from prospect theory to model uncertainty in human decisions. Unlike DPO, KTO requires only a binary feedback signal (desirable or undesirable) instead of explicit preference pairs.

extraction.title: Attention Is All You Need
extraction.summary: This paper introduces the Transformer, a neural network architecture based entirely on attention mechanisms without recurrence or convolutions. The model achieves state-of-the-art results on machine translation tasks (28.4 BLEU on WMT 2014 English-to-German and 41.8 BLEU on English-to-French) while being more parallelizable and requiring less training time than existing approaches. The Transformer is also demonstrated to generalize successfully to other tasks including English constituency parsing.

extraction.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
extraction.summary: This paper introduces GPT-3, a 175-billion-parameter autoregressive language model that demonstrates strong few-shot learning capabilities without fine-tuning. The authors show that scaling up language models improves task-agnostic performance across NLP benchmarks including translation, question-answering, and reasoning tasks, while also identifying limitations and societal implications of the model's ability to generate human-indistinguishable text.

extraction.title: AlphaFold
extraction.summary: AlphaFold is an AI program developed by DeepMind that uses deep learning to predict protein structures from amino acid sequences. The system has achieved breakthrough results, with AlphaFold 2 scoring above 90 on CASP's global distance test and AlphaFold 3 extending predictions to protein complexes with DNA, RNA, and ligands. The technology was recognized with the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for Demis Hassabis and John Jumper.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold is an AI program developed by DeepMind that uses deep learning techniques to predict protein structures.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold is an artificial intelligence (AI) program developed by DeepMind, a subsidiary of Alphabet, which performs predictions of protein structure. It is designed using deep learning techniques.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 1 placed first in the 13th CASP competition in December 2018.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold 1 (2018) placed first in the overall rankings of the 13th Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction (CASP) in December 2018.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 2 won the CASP14 competition in November 2020 with a global distance test score above 90 for approximately two-thirds of proteins.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold 2 (2020) repeated this placement in the CASP14 competition in November 2020. It achieved a level of accuracy much higher than any other entry. It scored above 90 on CASP's global distance test (GDT) for approximately two-thirds of the proteins.

key_claims.statement: The AlphaFold 2 paper published in Nature on 15 July 2021 had been cited nearly 43,000 times as of November 2025.
key_claims.supporting_quote: As of November 2025, the paper had been cited nearly 43,000 times.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 3 was announced on 8 May 2024 and can predict structures of complexes created by proteins with DNA, RNA, ligands, and ions.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold 3 was announced on 8 May 2024. It can predict the structure of complexes created by proteins with DNA, RNA, various ligands, and ions.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 3 shows a minimum 50% improvement in accuracy for protein interactions with other molecules compared to existing methods.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The new prediction method shows a minimum 50% improvement in accuracy for protein interactions with other molecules compared to existing methods.

key_claims.statement: Demis Hassabis and John Jumper shared one half of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for protein structure prediction.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Demis Hassabis and John Jumper shared one half of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, awarded "for protein structure prediction,"

key_claims.statement: David Baker won the other half of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for computational protein design.
key_claims.supporting_quote: while the other half went to David Baker "for computational protein design."

key_claims.statement: Over the last 60 years, experimental methods have identified the structures of about 170,000 proteins, while there are over 200 million known proteins across all life forms.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Such efforts, using the experimental methods, have identified the structures of about 170,000 proteins over the last 60 years, while there are over 200 million known proteins across all life forms.

key_claims.statement: CASP was launched in 1994 to challenge the scientific community to produce their best protein structure predictions.
key_claims.supporting_quote: CASP, which was launched in 1994 to challenge the scientific community to produce their best protein structure predictions

key_claims.statement: DeepMind trained AlphaFold on over 170,000 protein structures from the Protein Data Bank using between 100 and 200 GPUs.
key_claims.supporting_quote: DeepMind is known to have trained the program on over 170,000 protein structures from the Protein Data Bank, a public repository of protein sequences and structures. The overall training was conducted on processing power between 100 and 200 GPUs.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 2 uses a system of interconnected sub-networks forming a single, differentiable, end-to-end model based on pattern recognition, unlike AlphaFold 1 which used separately trained modules.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold 1 used a number of separately trained modules to produce a guide potential, which was then combined with a physics-based energy potential. AlphaFold 2 replaced this with a system of interconnected sub-networks, forming a single, differentiable, end-to-end model based on pattern recognition.

key_claims.statement: The AlphaFold Protein Structure Database was launched on July 22, 2021 as a joint project between AlphaFold and EMBL-EBI.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (AFDB), a joint project between AlphaFold and EMBL-EBI, was launched on July 22, 2021.

key_claims.statement: As of July 28, 2022, the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database contained the predicted structures of around 200 million proteins from 1 million species.
key_claims.supporting_quote: On July 28, 2022, the team uploaded to the database the structures of around 200 million proteins from 1 million species, covering nearly every known protein on the planet.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold has certain limitations, including providing models of individual protein chains rather than biological complexes and predicting low confidence scores for many protein regions.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold has shown certain limitations. - AlphaFold DB provides models of individual protein chains (monomers), rather than their biologically relevant complexes. - Many protein regions are predicted with low confidence score, including the intrinsically disordered protein regions.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold was used to predict structures of SARS-CoV-2 proteins whose structures were pending experimental detection in early 2020, with predictions reviewed by scientists at the Francis Crick Institute.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold has been used to predict structures of proteins of SARS-CoV-2, the causative agent of COVID-19. The structures of these proteins were pending experimental detection in early 2020. Results were reviewed by scientists at the Francis Crick Institute in the United Kingdom before being released to the broader research community.

key_claims.statement: Open-source code of AlphaFold 1 and 2 was released by DeepMind in 2022 after requests from the scientific community.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Open access to source code of several AlphaFold versions (excluding AlphaFold 3) has been provided by DeepMind in 2022 after requests from the scientific community.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 3 source code and weights were made publicly available in February 2025 with non-commercial restrictions.
key_claims.supporting_quote: It became publicly available in February 2025, still retaining the non-commercial restriction.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 2's results at CASP14 were described as 'astounding' and 'transformational' by members of the scientific community.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold 2's results at CASP14 were described as "astounding" and "transformational".

key_claims.statement: Some researchers noted that AlphaFold 2's accuracy was insufficient for a third of its predictions and that it did not reveal the underlying mechanism of protein folding.
key_claims.supporting_quote: However, some researchers noted that the accuracy was insufficient for a third of its predictions, and that it did not reveal the underlying mechanism or rules of protein folding for the protein folding problem, which remains unsolved.
